data.title: Granite Point Mortgage: I'm Adding To My High-Yield Preferred Stock Position
data.description:
Introduction

Granite Point Mortgage Trust (GPMT) focuses on originating and investing in floating rate commercial mortgage loans. The company has gone through a difficult past few years, and in my recent articles on Granite Point, I focused on its preferred shares, as those, of course, rank senior to the common securities. As it has already been a while since my previous article was published, I thought this is a good moment to have another look at Granite Point, before it publishes its FY 2025 earnings.

The results: the situation should be improving

Although the company uses the ‘distributable earnings’ (hereinafter ‘DE’) as its main performance metric, the calculation of the DE uses the reported net income as its starting point. We are still waiting for Granite Point to report its full-year result, I wanted to have a look back at the 9M 2025 results to get a better idea of where the company may be going from here.

In the third quarter of 2025, Granite Point reported a total interest income of $33.7M, which is almost a quarter lower compared to the Q3 2024 interest income. That’s not necessarily bad news, as the total amount of interest expenses decreased by about a third. And that means the net interest income increased by just over a third on a YoY basis. An excellent performance on a YoY basis, but it’s also a substantial improvement from the weaker performance in the first semester.

The income statement above also shows the increased revenue from real estate owned operations. Unfortunately, this still doesn’t cover the total real estate related expenses, and it explains once again why Granite Point prefers to have its loans in good standing over repossessing assets.

In any case, the company reported a profit of $3M (as it was also able to benefit from a reversal of loan loss provisions). And while that sounds positive, keep in mind the quarterly preferred dividend payments are costing the company approximately $3.6M, which means the bottom line result was a net loss attributable to the common shareholders of GPMT of $0.6M.

But as mentioned earlier in this article, the net income is less relevant than the distributable earnings are. Unfortunately, this didn’t make a big difference for GPMT as its DE came in at just $0.85M before recording about $19.8M in write-offs. This means the third quarter was indeed pretty bad, but perhaps the company is on the mend.

During its Q3 conference call, the GPMT management mentioned that it continues to resolve asset issues while retiring the higher-cost debt. Some of the troubled loans were—surprisingly—resolved, and in one case, a student housing loan was resolved at over $3M above the carrying value of the asset.

Additionally, Granite Point Mortgage now expects to start to grow its portfolio again in 2026, but based on the management comments, we shouldn’t expect this to happen in a meaningful way before the summer. That being said, Granite Point continues to pay its quarterly dividend of $0.05 per share.

At the end of the third quarter, GPMT had a total asset base of $1.8B, backed by $1.22B in liabilities and $582M in equity. As there are 8.23M preferred shares outstanding, for a total of $206M. This means the common equity offers an additional cushion of approximately $375M.

And although the total amount of equity on the balance sheet has decreased in the first nine months of the year, the balance sheet has gotten safer. The total asset base shrank by $310M while the total amount of liabilities decreased by $272M. At the end of Q3, the average LTV ratio at origination stood at 65%, with about 31.2% of the loans having an LTV ratio that exceeded 70% at origination.

At the end of September, offices still represented almost 42% of the entire loan portfolio, a decrease of 410 bps compared to the end of 2024. Meanwhile, the exposure to multifamily real estate increased, and on its Q3 conference call, the GPMT management indicated the middle market loan segment is quite favorable for that type of asset.

The preferred shares: attractive as rate reset comes closer

As explained in a previous article, GPMT currently has just one series of preferred shares, the Series A preferred shares (GPMT.PR.A).

These preferred shares were issued in November 2021 and currently offer a preferred dividend of $1.75 per year for a preferred dividend yield of 7% based on the $25 principal value of the preferred shares. The call date is November 30 of this year , and from then on, the preferred dividend gets updated to the three-month SOFR rate plus a markup of 583 basis points. A second interesting feature is that there's a floor: the minimum preferred dividend will be 7%, even if the three-month SOFR would be zero.

At the current share price of $20.35, the current yield is approximately 8.6%. Meanwhile, the 3M SOFR stands at 3.63%, which means the preferred dividend would reset to 3.63% + 5.83% = 9.46%, indicating a preferred dividend of $2.36 per share per year. And based on the current share price, that would indicate a current yield of approximately 11.6%.

If the SOFR rate remains high, Granite Point should call the preferred shares. But it may not have the balance sheet strength to do so. In that case, preferred shareholders could be in for a very nice surprise for the first few floating rate dividends. And even if the 3M SOFR rate were to drop to, say, 2%, the preferred stock would still yield over 9% based on its current share price.

Investment thesis

While the reported book value is just under $8/share, I think the common shares of Granite Point Mortgage are a ‘speculative buy’ at best, and that’s why I’m using a ‘buy’ rating at this point. This could be a high-risk/high-reward speculative investment, but I think we may need a few additional quarters before being certain.

I also think the preferred shares are a ‘buy’ at this point. Yes, the economy and the sector are still a bit on shaky ground, but I like the more senior securities a bit better.

I currently have a long position in Granite Point’s preferred shares. I have no position in the common shares issued by Granite Point but I am considering initiating a [small] speculative long position.

data.title: GM's Durability Discount: The Margin Proof The Market Needs (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

General Motors (GM) stock receives a durability discount compared with a more stable cash flow generator like Toyota (TM). The team at GM believes the company is far more durable than in the past, and there are key metrics to back up this assertion. So far this year, the valuation gap between the two companies has closed somewhat, but I believe that gap can close further in the years ahead if GM continues to make progress towards its goal of attaining a structural 8-10% adjusted EBIT margin range.

As I outlined in a past article, an 8-10% adj. EBIT margin would roughly double GM’s net income margin and drive greater automotive free cash flow generation. Because this would be underpinned by a structural improvement in its cost structure and market share gains with as much pricing power as an automaker can have, this might afford the company a higher multiple if this durability can be seen through a downtrend in auto sales.

My Take On Why The Market Doubts GM

GM has not been priced like a structurally resilient company with a sustainable business model. This is why the company consistently trades at a mid-to-high single-digit NTM P/E multiple, while a company like Toyota trades at a low-teens multiple. Why does TM earn such a higher multiple? Because the market believes its operating margins and free cash flows are durable, resilient to a recession, and that one really bad year won’t cause value destruction. The market has been right about this, as TM’s operating margin has held up better over downturns and reached higher peaks during boom times.

CFO Paul Jacobson has discussed how much more resilient GM has become, with an improved cost structure and consistent market-share gains. That’s exactly what the market needs to see to provide a more robust multiple. The market wants structurally robust operating margins, free cash flow generation, and through-cycle durability.

What must GM show the market to more meaningfully close the gap between its valuation profile and that of TM? I think the key lies in maintaining a structurally higher Adjusted EBIT margin and automotive free cash flow. In recent years, GM has effectively improved its cost structure, boosting operating margins and adjusted EBIT. Automotive free cash flow generation has followed.

What kind of multiple will GM earn if it maintains robust FCF margins and is a market leader in auto and truck sales?

What I’m Watching For In This Print

During this earnings print, I will be looking for the trajectory of adjusted EBIT margin, both reported and management’s commentary and confidence. If they reiterate the goal and have evidence of making progress, the thesis remains. GM has been offsetting tariffs and expects a tailwind in 2026, which must remain when the company reports Q4 earnings. I also want to see continued positivity around improving the warranty cost trend that impacted the company by over $1 billion in Q3. Additionally, the non-cash charges will have to slow down at some point, no surprises here will be welcomed.

These checkpoints are what will show progress toward the 8-10% adj. EBIT margin target, and show evidence that margins and FCF could be more resilient through the cycle. That’s our potential bridge to a higher multiple and narrowing the gap with Toyota.

Free Cash Flow Durability Improving

The first step in improving its FCF generation potential was to improve its cost structure by reducing SG&A to revenue. This fell from 8.5% ten years ago to just over 5.0% today.

Beyond SG&A, GM has benefited from a more favorable sales mix (full-size trucks/SUVs), disciplined incentives, and tighter working capital management versus prior cycles.

Since 2020, GM has built itself into a cash cow, with adj. Automotive FCF growing from $2.6 billion in FY 2020 to $9.7 billion over the TTM period, a CAGR of 31.7%. Meanwhile, reported GAAP and adjusted earnings have trailed the growth of FCF, with operating income up 4.2% annualized, and adj. EBIT up 5.3% annualized. The important factor in this is that GM has incurred significant charges for restructuring and onshoring, and pivoting from its EV strategy.

Because these charges are related to optimizing the profitability of the business, and offsetting tariffs, they appear to be margin accretive over the next few years, which should enable reported profitability to catch up to some extent. Not only is the cost structure improved, but GM now has several levers to pull to help it expand margins. Let’s dive a bit deeper into some of the key focus areas.

Lowering The Impact From Tariffs

Presidential Proclamation 10925 enables GM to effectively turn its incremental U.S. production into tariff credits. The proclamation provides 3.75% tariff offset credits in 2026 and 2.5% credits in 2027 for vehicles with final assembly in the USA. This offset allows the company to reduce the capitalized cost of its inventory, and is a reason GM lowered its projected 2025 tariff hit from $5 billion to a range of $3.5 to $4.5 billion.

Beyond reducing tariff exposure and profits, this could help GM protect its market share gains by offsetting tariff-caused price increases. The full benefit of this was not realized in 2025, so this will act as a marginal tailwind in 2026 and 2027. If you are looking for any nuggets during the earnings call, listen or look for discussions about tariff offsets and or greater or reduced tariff-related impacts (tailwinds/headwinds).

Warranty Expense Tailwind

GM expects a $1 billion tailwind from reduced warranty expenses in 2026. During Q3, GM reported a total warranty expense of $3.0 billion, well over the $1.7 billion in Q3 of 2024. To fix this problem, GM is shifting away from full transmission replacements towards component repairs, and this has already yielded cost savings. GM expects to see a benefit from this and other warranty cost reduction initiatives in 2026 and 2027. If this pressure truly does alleviate, it is a direct bridge to higher margins.

Onshoring Benefits and Shift From EVs

GM is investing heavily in onshoring, and shifting its production to higher-margin vehicles, like ICE vehicles, and full-size trucks. The onshoring will offset tariffs, and the shift from EVs will help improve automotive sales margins. This is coming with increased capital expenditures (cash outlays), and significant non-cash charges as the company right sizes the business. The cash outlays should normalize at some point, allowing for increased free cash flow. But the accounting earnings will be impacted by these charges in the short term, followed by a ramp of depreciation expense.

This is why adjusted EBIT is a better metric for measuring GM's operating performance.

In Q3, an EV strategic realignment of $1.592 billion, as well as OnStar litigation and Cruise restructuring charges, totaled a more significant adjustment than almost any of the last 8 quarters. Over the TTM period, adjusted EBIT has been impacted by nearly $10 billion in mostly non-recurring charges, which is why GAAP net income and EPS are not the best measurements of operational performance.

Earnings Expectations

When GM reports earnings on 1/27/2026, the consensus estimates expect revenue of$46.08 billion and adj. EPS of $2.26 per share.

GM has beaten estimates for 13 straight quarters, and another beat could spark a continuation of the rally the stock has experienced since it bottomed after the April 2025 tariff selloff and delivered better-than-feared earnings and raised guidance in both Q2 and Q3. This has pushed analyst estimates up over the last 90 days.

As it is, analysts are already expecting a 13.9% FY 2026 EPS boost to $11.83, over an expected FY 2025 EPS of $10.39. This puts GM at a 6.84x FY 2026 P/E. The high estimate reaches $15.12 per share. This is projected despite a meager 0.6% revenue increase expected in FY 2026, showing that the market now buys the optimized-for-profits GM thesis.

Valuation

GM currently trades at roughly an 8.3x TTM Adjusted Auto FCF. That multiple has increased from roughly 4x after it reported FY and Q4 2024 earnings.

GM currently trades at roughly 6.5x TTM adj. EBIT, but this is based on suboptimal margins.

With adj. EBIT margin currently at just 6.6%, an expansion into the 9.0% range (optimized margin profile) would deliver a normalized adjusted TTM EBIT of $16.9 billion, up from $12.4 billion, putting GM at roughly 4.8x TTM adj. EBIT. EBIT if the company were better optimized for profitability.

This is the core of the durability re-rating thesis. Higher through-cycle margins will destroy the ‘GM is a cyclical value trap’ narrative.

Risks

GM will not be able to meaningfully expand margins if auto demand stalls out or incentive-driven pricing strategies begin to show up in the company’s report.

If tariff offsets and credits either do not work out as expected or rules suddenly change, including higher tariffs or a rollback of offsets , the bridge to GM’s margin targets will, at best, be delayed.

Warranty risk still exists, if GM cannot improve quality control, the costs will hamper margin recovery and could impact sales growth.

Sometimes it takes the market longer than expected to come around to a turnaround or business improvement story. In the case of GM, which has already seen margins expand, the risk is that the market may wait for the actual results to show before granting the company a better multiple. With the overhang of tariffs, restructuring charges, and warranty costs still remaining, and estimates having been raised in recent weeks, it might be prudent to wait and see if GM stock suffers a near-term pullback before initiating a long position.

Takeaway

Despite a solid run-up in 2025, GM is still priced like a company with fragile margins. Long position holders or prospective buyers of GM stock should be looking at this earnings report and subsequent reports as durability tests. If GM can show continued progress toward its adj. EBIT margin goal, via a lower net tariff impact, a step-down in warranty costs, and fewer special items charges, the market will at some point get evidence that margins and automotive FCF are becoming more resilient through-the-cycle. That’s where the stock could bridge the valuation gap with Toyota. Any reacceleration of warranty or tariff headwinds, or surprise charges, might support the embedded cyclical narrative despite GM’s improved cost structure. I view this report as a potential catalyst for GM to rise further or to pull back meaningfully, offering a better buying point. While the stock is cheap, the valuation discount remains understandable. I rate GM a hold for now, with any sustainable acceleration in margin coupled with a guidance raise potentially upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

data.title: Apple, Microsoft, Meta To Report Results; Fed's Interest Rate Decision In Focus
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street was mixed on Friday as investors weighed global developments and central bank signals, capping a volatile week marked by disappointing earnings from Intel. From a sector standpoint, six of the S&P 500's 11 sectors were trading in positive trading territory with energy leading the way. In reverse, financials have been the weakest performer. President Donald Trump on Thursday said the U.S. would essentially have "total access" to Greenland under the deal that is being negotiated with NATO, according to an interview with Fox Business.

As tech earnings take centre stage in the coming week, the Federal Reserve's interest rate decision will dominate the economic calendar. The first half of the week is expected to be comparatively lighter, with consumer confidence data releasing on Tuesday and Donald Trump's speech due on Wednesday. The Fed's rate decision will be out on Thursday, along with initial jobless claims. On Friday, PPI data for December and the Chicago PMI are scheduled to be released.

The week ending January 30 will see major S&P 500 companies, including Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), Tesla (TSLA), Boeing (BA), Visa (V), and Mastercard (MA), releasing their quarterly financial results.

_______________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday: Boeing, UnitedHealth (UNH), Texas Instruments (TXN), RTX Corp (RTX), General Motors (GM). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday: Microsoft, Meta Platforms, Tesla, IBM (IBM), AT&T (T). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday: Apple, Visa A, Mastercard, Caterpillar (CAT). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday: Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), American Express (AXP), Verizon (VZ). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: AppLovin (APP) and Salesforce (CRM) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include ImmunityBio (IBRX), Corvus Pharmaceuticals (CRVS), and Masonglory (MSGY). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Abbott (ABT), Netflix (NFLX), and Diginex (DGNX).

IPO watch: Brazilian fintech upstart Picpay (PICS) and insurance tech company Ethos Technologies (LIFE) are expected to price their IPOs and begin to trade. The IPO lockup period expires for certain blocks of shares of Ambiq Micro (AMBQ) and Shoulder Innovations (SI).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Clorox (CLX), Paychex (PAYX), Costco (COST), and Morgan Stanley (MS).

Apple Earnings Preview: Apple is expected to post first-quarter earnings of $2.67 per share and revenue of $138.47B. Apple had earlier projected double-digit growth for iPhone sales, with analysts' consensus pegged at 13.6%. HSBC Research said that Apple led the global smartphone market in 2025 with 20% market share, largely driven by the success of the iPhone 17 series, in particular, iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max. The company's product line remains strong with the possible launch of a foldable phone, smart glasses and a special 20th anniversary edition iPhone model with curved glass, it added. However, it noted that the firm has been late to the AI game. "We see this as a sign that Apple is prioritising faster timelines over full AI stack independence," analysts at the brokerage said.

Meta Earnings Preview: Meta's fourth-quarter earnings are projected at $8.19 per share with earnings of $58.40B. HSBC said that it sees the company benefiting from its early involvement in AI models and a very high investment. AI already effectively supports Meta's advertising business by driving higher user engagement and, in turn, expanding ad space, the broker noted. "We see Meta growing at a faster pace than its addressable market over the next few years (with an estimated 31.6% market share by 2030), driven by leverage of AI to improve ad results and algorithms, as well as higher engagement driven by growth in impressions, pricing, and volumes," it said. Additional growth drivers include WhatsApp monetisation and leadership in smart glasses, analysts said.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UTF: This Fund Should Finally Begin To Outperform The S&P 500 (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The term the past is prologue is often important for investors. While history seldom repeats itself, obviously, historical events usually set the stage for the future. In order to invest for the future, people need to have an understanding of what has happened in the past.

Even though the recent annual rate of inflation of 2.7 percent came in ahead of the Fed's stated target of 2 percent, the central bank is likely to continue to ease rates with job growth slowing and middle- and low-income consumers still struggling with high prices and above-average debt levels. The current, more dovish rate cycle should remain for some time.

Two sectors that should benefit significantly from what is likely to be an extended cycle of lower rates are the utility and infrastructure industries. These sectors tend to carry more debt and also often be more capital intensive; lower borrowing costs usually give companies in these industries significantly more financial flexibility.

One of the better-performing investments that is focused on the utility and infrastructure sectors is the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF). UTF is also a leveraged fund that relies in part on variable rate financing to provide investors with additional income while maximizing total returns as well.

The Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund has offered investors total returns of 228.1 percent over the last 10 years, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 327 percent during this same time period.

Still, has significantly underperformed the S&P 500 for much of the last 3 years as rates remained higher.

UTF offered investors total returns of 27 percent over the last 3 years, while the S&P 500 offered investors total returns of 81 percent during this same time period.

I last wrote about the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund in April of 2025, when I upgraded the investment to a hold. I am upgrading this investment to a buy today. The utility sector should continue to benefit from the increased data usage that the move to artificial intelligence will need, and rates should continue to move lower over the next year. Recent economic data suggests the economy should continue to grow slowly. UTF is well-positioned to offer investors consistent income and total returns in this kind of financial environment, with inflation and interest rates likely to remain lower.

The Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund has an expense ratio of 2.29 percent, $2.54 billion in assets under management, and a current yield of 7.43 percent. The expense ratio includes the cost of leverage, which is obtained at fixed and floating financial rates. Almost 77 percent of the fund's financing is with fixed rates. This closed-ended fund's current leverage rate is 29.21 percent.

UTF's holdings are 54.32 percent utilities, 20.77 percent industrials, 18.97 percent energy, 5.95 percent real estate, and 16.28 percent in corporate bonds. The CEF's four largest positions are NextEra Energy (NEE), TC Energy Corp (TRP), National Grid PLC (NGG), and NiSource (NI). These holdings make up nearly 15 percent of the overall fund's assets. This investment's energy holdings are largely focused on midstream companies whose profitability levels aren't as susceptible to price swings.

The outlook for the utility sector in 2025 is strong. The AI Data center market is expected to continue to grow at a double-digit rate. The move to increasing new forms of energy to support infrastructure, including electrification, and increasing investment in US manufacturing with the President's tariffs and tax incentives in the 2025 budget, should all benefit this sector. Lower rates also benefit this higher debt industry as well, since the sector is also more capital-intensive, and financing projects such as updating the grids of companies will be more advantageous.

The current rate cycle should also continue for some time. While the economy remains stronger for upper-income individuals, there are continued signs that middle- and low-income consumers are struggling. Recent economic data shows that spending at the higher end has held up well. Multiple companies, including McDonald's (MCD) and Walmart (WMT), have recently stated that working-class people are facing tougher choices and frequently trading down. The Fed has also recently discussed how significant increases in spending on discount items at thrift stores also show a bifurcated economy where the average family is still struggling. Credit card debt remains near record high levels as well. Finally, when President Trump appoints a new Fed Chair in May of this year, the new head of the central bank is also likely to be more dovish on rates, given the President's recent clashes with Powell.

The Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund has more flexibility in a lower rate environment to use leverage to offer investors consistent distributions as well.

UTF has paid out distributions of nearly $0.155 a month, and the investment has paid out $1.86 of income over the last year without having to frequently rely on return of capital. The closed-end fund's current yield is projected to be between 7 and 8 percent annually. While this investment has not usually increased the annual payouts recently, distributions have remained consistent.

All investment theses have risks; if inflation rates were to accelerate because of new tariffs and the current rate cycle ended, the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund would likely face additional headwinds. The upcoming midterm elections in the US also create some financial and regulatory uncertainty, since if Democrats were to win, the party may move to limit some of the tax breaks for industry Republicans passed in 2025. Energy prices have also been moving lower over the last year, and significant new supply is supposed to come online in 2026, which could put pressure on UTF's midstream holdings as well.

The Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund predictably underperformed the major indexes by a wide margin during the recent inflationary period when rates were moving higher, but this CEF is much better positioned today. Indeed, with consensus economic estimates expecting growth of just 2 percent next year, equities should offer more muted returns moving forward. While UTF has underperformed the broader indexes over most of the last two decades, this CEF should also be better positioned moving forward to outperform the benchmarks as well.

data.title: Above The Noise: Look Beyond Distracting Headlines
data.description:
By Brian Levitt, Chief Global Market Strategist and Head of Strategy & Insights

Hall of Fame football coach Bill Parcells, during the New York Giants’ 1986 title run, was asked by a reporter if he was scared to play Washington for the third time. Parcells replied, “Let me tell you what I’m scared of: Snakes, spiders, and the IRS.”

I’m so often asked what scares me about the ongoing market advance that I’ve been tempted to borrow Parcells’ line.

Venezuela? A small economy.1 Iran? Same.2

Federal Reserve (Fed) independence? It’s critical, but I won’t be concerned unless inflation expectations break meaningfully above current levels.3

Greenland? Tariffs? It’s not ideal, but we’ve navigated tariffs before. Following Liberation Day, a “yippie” bond market had the Trump administration pausing tariffs, and the S&P 500 Index posting its third-best one-day return in 30 years.4

So, what does scare me?

It’s not snakes and spiders. I’ll be concerned when signs emerge of excessive leverage building across the US economy, inflation expectations drifting meaningfully outside the Fed’s comfort zone, a clear rollover in economic activity, credit spreads blowing out, and bankers tightening lending standards.5

None of that’s happening today.

Until it does, I remain bullish on stocks, despite the incessant noise in the headlines and the ongoing geopolitical maneuvering.

It may be confirmation bias, but…

… the much‑anticipated broadening of the US stock market does appear to be taking hold. This pattern is typical as the Fed eases policy and economic activity improves. Historically, during easing cycles, the equal‑weight S&P 500 Index (a proxy for broader market participation) has tended to outperform the market‑cap‑weighted index. (See chart below.) Early signs suggest this dynamic is emerging. Market cap has still outperformed equal weight over the past year, but that gap has been narrowing.6

US stock market broadening appears to be taking hold

S&P 500 Equal Weight Index vs. S&P 500 Index: Rolling 1-year return differential and 1-year change in fed funds rate

It was said (part 1)

“Kevin’s too good on television to send to the Fed.” — President Donald Trump7

President Trump’s recent comments about Kevin Hassett, long viewed as the perceived front‑runner for the Fed Chair role, effectively reopened the competition for the top job, with candidates such as Kevin Warsh and Christopher Waller emerging as leading contenders. Alas, my name still hasn’t appeared on any lists. I’m beginning to think I’m not in the running for the final rose!

As we’ve incessantly said, Fed independence remains critical. It has been modestly concerning to see inflation expectations drift higher in January, even if they’re within the Fed’s comfort zone.8 Investors should remember that the next Fed Chair will not wield unilateral control, because the governors generally lack deep political ties, the Biden‑appointed members remain in place, and Trump appointees like Waller and Michelle Bowman have been voting in line with the current Fed. This bears watching (pun intended!), as rising concerns over Fed independence represent the primary downside risk to our outlook. Nonetheless, we continue to expect the institution’s integrity to hold.

It was said (part 2)

“Europe owns Greenland, it also owns a lot of Treasuries.” — George Saravelos, Deutsche Bank FX Strategist9

George Saravelos makes an interesting argument. There’s $3.5 trillion of US Treasury debt held in Europe, which is 9.8% of US outstanding debt.10 In fact, Europe owns far more than China, even though Chinese ownership of US debt has long been viewed as a powerful potential geopolitical tool.

It’s important to keep this in perspective, however. There’s not going to be a mass sale of US assets by European governments.

  • First, most of the US assets held in Europe are owned by the private sector, including insurance companies, pension plans, banks, and individual citizens. (The Danish pension fund announced that it will divest itself of $100 million in US Treasuries, representing 0.4% of its account and 0.00026% of total US Treasury debt.11)
  • Second, they’d be selling against themselves, because it’s unclear who’d be willing to take the other side of such a trade.
  • Third, the US remains the largest, deepest, and most liquid market in the world, which makes reallocating elsewhere extremely difficult.

The conclusion is that the current debate over Greenland doesn’t point to a sudden liquidation of US Treasuries. Instead, we maintain our view that investors will likely gradually diversify away from US assets, which suggests continued weakness in the US dollar. This supports our expectation that international assets will likely outperform in 2026.

Think/rethink

Think: Geopolitical events have a meaningful impact on the stock market.

Rethink: The average one-year return of the S&P 500 Index following a peak in the Geopolitical Risk Index is 15.40%.12

Phone a friend

Why are Japanese bond yields surging, and should investors be concerned? I posed the question to Rob Waldner, Chief Strategist and Head of Macro Research for Invesco Fixed Income. His response:

“Japanese bond yields13 have jumped on the back of a much larger‑than‑expected fiscal package and fears that the Bank of Japan (BoJ) has been too slow to raise rates, despite the December rate hike. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s stimulus, equivalent to nearly 3% of gross domestic product (GDP),14 raised concerns about increased bond issuance and future fiscal expansion, pushing yields higher.

Markets now view the central bank as behind the curve, a perception reinforced by the unusual combination of rising rates and a weak currency.15 This dynamic is likely to be resolved but will likely entail increased market volatility, including further increases in Japanese bond yields, until the BoJ is forced to tighten more aggressively. That would then likely be followed by stabilization and a rally in the yen.”

On the road again

My travels took me to Phoenix, where I was on an agenda with the technology and science writer and CBS News Correspondent David Pogue. David’s session focused on artificial intelligence (AI), and while he acknowledged the many concerns surrounding tech, he reminded us that society has always feared new innovations. Remember when it was believed that traveling 50 miles per hour on a steamship would drive a person mad?

David made clear that although AI will bring challenges that require thoughtful regulation, the potential benefits are likely to be extraordinary. He delivered on his promise to end with mind-blowing examples, ranging from AI interpreting a confusing parking sign to David Beckham appearing in an advertisement speaking multiple languages he doesn’t know.

But the example that truly struck me was Google AI releasing structural predictions for nearly 200 million proteins known to science. When you know the structure of a protein thought to be involved in disease, it increases the probability of discovering a drug molecule that will bind to it and alter its behavior, hopefully curing or mitigating the disease. Before this breakthrough, it’s estimated that scientists had known the structure of only 0.085% of all known proteins. David believes this development will unlock transformational medical breakthroughs, including treatment and cures for many of the most debilitating illnesses.

data.title: The Slowest Train Crash: How To Protect Your Retirement From 2026 Economic Realities
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

One of the more popular soundbites from Warren Buffett is the caution to "Be fearful when others are greedy." Yet like many tidbits of good investing advice, it is widely ignored. It is kind of like "buy low, sell high." Common sense, yet the evidence suggests that the vast majority of investors fail to follow it.

Today, we have an example of greed in the stock market that, in my opinion, is blindingly obvious. Let's define "greed," because investors trying to make the most money is "greed" in this context. When we say "greedy," we mean investors who are investing without paying attention to the fundamentals supporting the investment, such as earnings, economic conditions, and valuations.

In other words, investors are "blinded by greed" and not considering the overall risk because they are buying equities primarily due to them going up in price, as opposed to buying equities because there is a fundamentally strong economy and they are at a good valuation.

Let's take a look at some of the fundamentals that the market is apparently ignoring.

Jobs, Jobs, Jobs

The monthly jobs report used to be something that was very important in explaining market movements. Jobs are an important fundamental for businesses in a number of different ways, including but not limited to:

  • Hiring more people is often an indicator that businesses are expanding and growing. Laying off people can increase earnings through savings and increased efficiencies, but these improvements are short-term relative to expansion. A business can always expand more, but firing employees only improves efficiency and earnings to a point.
  • Consumers with jobs spend more money than consumers without jobs. Seems like common sense, but alas, common sense isn't always common. It doesn't really matter how great your product is if your consumers can't/won't pay for it.
  • Rising unemployment has historically been the best indicator of an imminent recession.

So, it is very natural to see the S&P 500 (SPX) do something like this:

From 2006-2008, the S&P 500 went up while unemployment was declining. Almost as soon as unemployment started climbing, the S&P 500 topped off. Then it crashed as unemployment rose.

Here is a look at 1999-2001:

Again, the S&P 500 peaked while unemployment was at a bottom, and actually started to materially decline before unemployment went up. In fact, by the time unemployment went above 4.2%, the S&P 500 was already in a bear market.

This is something that people with no financial experience would look at and say, "Duh, that's common sense." If unemployment goes up, the stock market should generally be expected to decline.

Defying Gravity

Yet, here we are in 2026, and for the past three years, unemployment has gone up in a relatively steady trajectory. The market's response? Going up in the same steady trajectory:

That isn't normal, and it defies common sense.

What's different? The most obvious thing is the pace of the increase. Unemployment has been climbing, but it hasn't shot up suddenly like we saw in either the dot-com recession or the Great Financial Crisis. Instead, it has been more of a slow and steady march up, which has accumulated to be a very significant 100 bps increase over three years.

That slow increase hasn't been enough to spook the market. For a few years now, we've faced gradually deteriorating job reports that objectively are "not horrible" and often beat the expectations. Never mind that the expectations are very low; expectations being beaten is enough for the market to ignore bad results.

Jobs Report: Still Not Horrible

The December Current Employment Situation Reports (aka "Jobs Report") came out in January, and it made the market very confident that the Fed won't cut rates right now. Unemployment ticked down slightly to 4.4%, and the jobs added came in at 50k. Neither are great numbers, but they do fall into the category of "not horrible." Source

Of course, the 50k in jobs added came along with the usual revisions. October was revised down 68k, and November was revised down 8k.

Essentially, the number of jobs has been flat since April 2025, with the gross number in December just 92k higher than the number posted in April 2025. It's a number that is well within the standard of error, as we just saw a 76k downward revision. Source

The current count for the year in 2025 was 584k, which compares to slightly over 2 million in 2024. Yet, those numbers that we've seen quoted in numerous news articles ignore the fact that 491k of that increase occurred from January to April.

If we look at the year-over-year growth, 0.376% is a bad number. The U.S. population is growing faster than 0.376%. In March 2008, it was 0.344%, and in April 2001, it was 0.435%—numbers that are consistent with an ongoing recession. Source

This is a real problem for the U.S. economy and should be a real problem for the stock market. This is the slowest train crash the U.S. economy has ever seen, but that doesn't change the reality that the train is crashing.

In economics, trends matter, and the trend has been negative for four years now. It isn't at all clear what will turn this trend around. It is even less clear when the Federal Reserve and the stock market will become concerned about it. For now, investors are greedy, and they aren't pricing in any serious risk of a recession.

Valuations Remain High

The valuations for the S&P 500 remain at multi-decade highs. While the S&P 400 MidCaps and S&P 600 SmallCaps are trading close to average valuations: Source

Large caps were more expensive during the dot-com bubble, but it is worth noting that they were higher in 1999 and early 2000, before unemployment started rising.

From a macro-perspective, what we now recognize as a "bubble" made some sense. Unemployment was below 4%, the economy by every measure was very strong, and there was a lot to be optimistic about. We now recognize that optimism was irrational exuberance with the benefit of 20/20 hindsight. But at the time, bulls could pull out all sorts of really strong economic data to support their outlook. So, while the market was overly exuberant, at least there was some objectively strong data to support it.

Today, where is the strong economic data? It doesn't exist. At best, we can look at the jobs report and say, "It could be worse."

Conclusion

There is a very clear disconnect between Wall Street and Main Street. Valuations shouldn't be at multi-decade highs at the same time that unemployment has been climbing for years. The economy hasn't collapsed yet, but not collapsing is different from being really strong. We look back at the Dot-com bust and say, "What were people thinking? It was obviously a bubble!" But when valuations were that high, GDP was 4.5%-5%.

Today, we cheer one quarter of growth at a rate over 4%, which almost certainly won't translate to a full year of +4% growth, as we routinely saw in the late 1990s.

We don't know if 2026 is the year that things fall apart. What we do know is that the market is being greedy. Investors are ignoring the economic fundamentals and are willing to buy their favorite stocks regardless of what the price is. Warren Buffett was exactly right to warn investors to be fearful when others are greedy, and we're telling you that others are greedy.

How do you protect your portfolio?

  • Make sure you have a healthy allocation to fixed income and other cash-producing investments. Cash flow is a powerful tool for your portfolio that will provide you with the flexibility to adapt when times change.
  • Focus on valuations when you pick stocks. The stock market is expensive, but not everything in the market is expensive. There are some great opportunities even in large caps. For example, we like Verizon (VZ) yielding 7% with a P/E ratio under 10x.
  • Explore opportunities among small caps. Small caps are trading at the lowest valuations right now and make up a sizable portion of our portfolio. If you want quick exposure to small caps while you take time to identify individual opportunities, consider a proven fund like Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT), which pays out a variable distribution based on an annualized 7% rate of the trailing four quarters' NAV. RVT focuses on small caps, and here is how it did during the Dot-com bubble bust.

The market isn't always a rational place, and it is easy to get wrapped up in greed because the tickers that are at the highest valuations are the tickers that have generated the most returns in recent years.

Yet, at the end of the day, we are investing in businesses that operate within the economy. They are impacted by economic realities, and when valuations become high even as the economy slows, that should be a warning sign. Heed it. Make sure your portfolio is prepared for it, and make sure your retirement survives it.

At High Dividend Opportunities, our members are prepared. Are you?

data.title: Telos: Security Solutions And Telos ID Drive The Path To Profitability
data.description:
Telos Corporation (TLS) has really struggled over the last few years, especially on the revenue and margin fronts, but I’ve seen really promising signs of a turnaround in 2025. Over the first three quarters of the year, the company recorded steady growth in its numbers, and Q3 2025 is its best operating quarter over the last 5 years, as far as I can tell. From a purely financial perspective, I’d say it has found its footing again.

But that improvement also creates a bit of a problem, because the stock has re-rated sharply (up 74%) over the last year, especially after the Q2 results came out. It is still less expensive than it was in 2022 and 2023, but we’re seeing signs that the market is already buying into its turnaround after just one big quarter. As such, I want to lay out my thoughts on whether the business can grow into the heightened expectations from investors.

Telos: Business Overview

Telos is a federal-focused cybersecurity and IT services provider that operates through two reporting segments. Here’s how the company describes itself, per its website:

“Telos Corporation is the leading provider of cyber, cloud and enterprise security solutions for the world’s most security-conscious organizations…The company serves military, intelligence and civilian agencies of the federal government, allied nations and commercial organizations around the world.”

That’s a relatively broad overview, but I’ll briefly touch on its business segments below.

The first one is Security Solutions, and it covers everything from cyber risk management and compliance automation to identity and credentialing and secure messaging. The company’s products include Xacta, which helps government agencies manage compliance and authorization workflows, and Telos ID, which supports identity-related programs such as TSA PreCheck enrollment. The 2025 rebound I mentioned earlier is largely a result of the segment’s performance, given the US government’s policies around security and immigration over the last year. So, it has benefited from larger federal programs and higher-margin work as a whole.

Then, we have Secure Networks. This part of Telos’s business focuses on secure network architecture, secure mobility, and network defense services for mission-critical environments. It is more services-heavy, labor-intensive, and has structurally lower margins because of its very nature, and it has also been the weaker spot during the company’s downturn. That’s mostly because it wasn’t winning new awards and contracts as quickly as it was wrapping up the legacy programs.

So, Security Solutions is the main growth and leverage driver for the company, and Secure Networks is still a swing factor in the overall story. Next, I’ll discuss Telos’s growth catalysts and risks.

Customers, Positioning, And Growth Levers

Heavy US Federal Exposure

If you just read the company’s description of itself, you might think that it is a major hotshot globally, but that isn’t really the case. It is still overwhelmingly a US federal contractor, and I don’t see any signs that that will change anytime soon. The good part is that having the federal government as a customer means that you’re almost assured of consistent program awards for an extended period. The not-so-great part is that the revenue recognition is never as smooth as it could be because of things like program timing, task orders, recompetes, and general procurement bureaucracy.

We can see both sides of this advantage with the company’s customer concentration figures from Q3 2025: 92% of its revenue came from federal customers. That concentration gives it a moat because federal cyber buyers typically prioritize compliance, track record, and delivery history. Conversely, the same federal buyers can pause or reshape programs for reasons that have nothing to do with Telos’ execution, which is something it has suffered from in the recent past.

Now, if we zoom out a bit, I think there is a nice tailwind from macro conditions. For one, Deltek forecasts that the federal IT market spend will rise from $131.0 billion in FY 2024 to $156.1 billion in FY 2028. I believe that Telos’s growth in 2025 is a sign that it is benefiting from the administration’s policies and general market conditions. The growth may not be even, but it is supposed to be steady.

However, there may be some headwinds around contracting scrutiny and what I’d call “show me ROI.” President Trump’s administration seems to be all for cutting government spending across the board, although I’m not too sure about how much success his DOGE team had on that front in 2025. If administrations and agencies start reviewing spending, the easiest contracts to pressure are the ones that look like generic consulting or loosely defined services. In Telos’ case, it is still possible for its business to get squeezed if it looks like overhead.

One more thing. Given the government shutdown in October and November last year, I think this may have a significant bearing on the company’s earnings in Q4. That’s nearly 6 weeks of a 12/13-week quarter impacted by government operations not running, which means contract awards, payouts, and other funding stalling or missing their intended deadlines, among other things. Management says they already captured this in their outlook for the quarter, but we’ll see.

Compliance Automation & FedRAMP Are Real Differentiators

Compliance is a budget line for most government agencies, and federal cloud adoption is expanding. There’s a problem there, because compliance friction is slowing the latter down. Now, Telos has Xacta, a platform that handles cyber risk management and compliance automation. Ideally, the platform should reduce the friction with compliance because it aligns with how federal authorization and continuous monitoring actually work.

The main thing I’m positive about here is that Xacta has FedRAMP High Authorization for its SaaS offering, which means that Telos can now support more workloads in the cloud, especially for agencies that work with high-impact data and systems. This is fantastic for the company because it lowers the adoption friction significantly for most agencies. Besides, the FedRAMP program is actively modernizing its processes, and it made record authorization volumes in 2025. Of course, this means that cloud providers, along with the vendors that help government agencies manage their compliance evidence, controls mapping, and continuous monitoring, will all benefit.

I must point out, of course, that budget reality is something to watch out for. According to GovWin, federal civilian departments have a cybersecurity budget of ¬$12 billion for FY26, and some agencies have higher or lower spending allowances this year than previously. Meanwhile, we may see federal cyber funding flatline in some appropriations areas. So, Telos is competing with many other IT companies for these federal budgets in specific areas, and its offerings need to continue being the best possible options for compliance and risk management if it is going to keep enjoying the rebound over the past year.

Identity and Enrollment Services Have Real Demand Behind Them

The final piece of Telos’s growth is identity via its Telos ID platform. The company is a TSA PreCheck enrollment provider, and that is a growth lever that a lot of its competitors in federal cyber contracting don’t have. There’s structural demand here, given that the business is directly linked to a consumer-facing program. The TSA PreCheck program had over 20 million active members in 2025, and Telos now has 504 locations in the US and Puerto Rico.

The company is on to a winner here, especially when you consider the other tailwinds around travel in general. More travelers, especially in the US, appreciate the convenience and time-saving that pre-checking offers, and travel volumes are normalizing again. If we take those together, it is reasonable to assume that the registration volumes will compound even more, which directly improves Telos’s business because identity doesn’t depend on government recompete cycles.

However, enrollment is a scale business that depends on footprint, throughput, staffing, and customer experience, so it isn’t a high-margin SaaS product like Xacta. I believe it will be a strong revenue driver moving forward, especially when you hear management talking about an additional $28 million in revenue coming from Telos ID in FY26.

The Numbers Show The Recovery Is Program-Driven

Telos made ~$118 million in sales from Q1 to Q3 FY2025, up 44% YoY. That’s impressive, but I want to look at what each segment actually contributed. Security Solutions brought in ~$104.8 million, up 91% YoY, and that overperformance was mostly because of expanding its Telos ID business. On the other hand, Security Networks’ revenue dropped 51% YoY to $13.3, so that is a 90-10 ratio split in favor of the former. Obviously, the growth is highly concentrated in one segment, and we need to watch out for how that mix continues to evolve over the next few quarters.

On the margin side, the picture is really good, too. Gross profit for the 9-month period was $44.7 million, and gross margin was 37.8%, which is an 8.7 percentage-point increase from the same period in FY2024. It looks even better if you only compare the Q3 numbers year over year, as you can see in the image below.

Operating margin is where I’m most impressed, because the company went from negative 27.5% in Q2 FY25 to negative 5% in Q3 FY25, and the contrast is even sharper if you compare it to Q3 FY24’s -70.5%. That’s mostly because operating expenses fell slightly over the same period while revenue rose by 116%. So, the company’s operating leverage is much better, and for me, that is a meaningful change in trajectory.

Then, cash flow. Telos generated ~$22 million in operating cash flow across Q1-Q3 FY25, which is a ~$37 million swing from the same timeframe in FY24, and it ended the period with just over $59 million in cash. That’s a big change, and now the company has breathing room and downside protection that didn’t exist a year ago.

I also like the visibility into revenue for Q4 and beyond. According to the Q3 Form 10-Q, it has a funded backlog of $65.5 million, and management expects to recognize 97% of that as revenue over the next 12 months, including Q4. Contract liabilities were also double what they were at the end of FY2024, so things are looking up.

Valuation

I valued Telos using its forward sales multiple, and it looks as compelling as I would expect from a Buy perspective.

First, the multiples. The company expects to make ~$163 million in revenue in FY25, and management is also guiding for at least $180 million from its existing programs in FY26. I’ll be generous and add a 10% step-up for the program wins it expects from Xacta AI and new business opportunities within its multibillion-dollar pipeline, per management’s words during the Q3 earnings call. That takes FY26’s revenue up to ~$200 million.

The company currently trades at 2.3x EV/Sales, which is what I’d consider a base case, and I’ll apply a slightly higher multiple for a bull case scenario. Here’s how the valuation comes together using these multiples:

Assumption

Value (base case)

Value (bull case)

FY2026 Revenue

Implied Share Price

The upside from today’s price is barely 40 cents, but we must remember that there are multiple tailwinds that are pushing the business’s growth. I believe that if the business performs even remotely close to those topline expectations, it is very likely that the market will quickly re-rate its sales multiple to the 3.5 or 4x range, and that represents nearly 100% upside from the $5.8/share it is trading at now.

Also, the company isn’t too far off breakeven and eventual profitability. Given what the last few quarters have been like, I’ll stick my neck out and say that it can be profitable as soon as the middle of FY27. It could be sooner, but I wouldn’t be so sure. If that happens, I’m very confident that the market will start assigning a premium to the stock’s price.

The Risks To My Position

I think the most obvious risk to any bullishness around Telos is its customer concentration. I touched on this before, but I will reiterate it again. The company’s entire business is basically built around federal government contracts and awards, for the most part. That is a moat, yes, but it is also risky, as the government shutdown and policies like tariffs have shown us. That volatility is a big part of why the stock has traded so cheaply in recent years, and the higher the value, the more important it is that it has some level of earnings stability. That should come with the ID side of its business, so I’m not too bothered at this time.

Revenue lumpiness is next, and it ties into my previous point. We saw the ramp in revenue when Security Solutions started picking up, especially in Q3, but the dynamic cuts both ways. One major program going offline will dent the company’s revenue outlook, and it needs to keep working to add more customers from the billion-dollar pipeline that management is so bullish on.

The segments also need to balance out. Right now, the revenue mix between Security Solutions and Security Networks is 90:10, and that is working out. But it also means that Telos is relying on a fairly narrow set of growth drivers, so any dip in the former’s growth trajectory will be particularly impactful if Security Networks doesn’t start picking up some slack.

I mentioned that Q3 FY25 was the company’s best quarter in a long time in terms of operating margins, but some of that improvement came from easier YoY comparisons. The real test now is whether those margins will hold up if the volumes fluctuate and new work comes in under different contract structures. It’s not a risk I can discount because it plays a big role in how we price the stock moving forward.

The last risk I must highlight is the valuation itself. The stock has already rallied very nicely, and the market expects that the company will continue performing at the current levels. So, Telos doesn’t need to miss badly for us to see the downside. One or two “okay” quarters might just do it.

Final Thoughts

Telos has done the hard part, and it is recovering nicely in terms of its financials. I also like its strategy and the tailwinds that will power it, so the question is whether it can grow into the valuation the market seems to be expecting. I believe it will, especially if it just gives us a few more quarters of consistent execution, so I’m willing to have some faith in the upside and rate the stock a Buy.

data.title: RIV: Fund Of Funds, 13% Yield, Distribution Hike
data.description:
We previously covered RiverNorth Opportunities Fund (RIV), a closed-end fund, in an article in early September 2025. At that time, we rated RIV a Hold due to a history of distribution declines and lagging performance.

However, on 1/2/26, RIV raised its monthly distribution by 2.3%, from $.1277 to $.1306, its first distribution hike since January 2024. At its 1/22/26 closing price of $11.89, RIV yields a hefty 13.18%. Its next two monthly ex-dividend dates are 2/13/26 and 3/16/26.

Fund Profile

"The Fund employs a tactical asset allocation strategy primarily comprised of closed-end funds, exchange-traded funds, special purpose acquisition companies (SPACs), and business development companies (BDCs). RiverNorth implements an opportunistic investment strategy designed to capitalize on the inefficiencies in the closed-end fund space while simultaneously providing diversified exposure to several asset classes." (RIV site)

RIV's net base increased from $275M at 6/30/25, to $343M as of 1/21/26, due to a rights offering, which sold 5,899,015 new shares of common stock at a subscription price of $11.95. Its expense ratio was steady, while leverage decreased from 32% to 24.42% as of 1/21/26. Quantity of holdings was up slightly, from 339 to 341, and average daily volume was 94K. RIV incepted in December 2015:

Holdings

As of 12/31/25, special purpose acquisition companies, "SPACs", remained the top exposure, rising from 26% to 28%, with fixed income declining from 19% to 16%, and US equities steady, at 20%. Investment company bonds dropped from 13% to 12%, while international equities rose from 6% to 7%.

The biggest shift in assets came in BDCs, which rose from 2% to 6%, as of 12/31/25 - a contrarian move, with rates generally forecast to decline.

CEFs dropped from 45% at 6/30/25 to 43% as of 12/31/25, with investment company debt up from 13% to 14%, while other categories remained steady.

As of 9/30/25, the US was by far RIV's main regional exposure, at ~63% in equity and 28% in fixed income. It held minor positions in several other nations, running from 0.32% for Germany up to 1.32% for Hong Kong:

RIV's portfolio remained focused upon investment grade securities, but at a lower rate, as AAA - BBB-rated holdings comprised ~58% of the portfolio, vs. 63% as of 3/31/25. RIV also held ~23% in unrated securities, plus ~15.8% in non-investment grade securities:

And 3-5 year maturities still had the highest weighting, at 11.87%, vs. 12.36% at 3/31/25, while 10-20 year maturities declined from 10.24% to 5.37% as of 9/30/25. 1-3 year maturities fell from 5.25% to 3.5%.

As of 9/30/25, RIV's top 10 holdings were still headed by Bill Ackman's Pershing Square Holdings, at 12.66%. It's "structured as a closed-ended fund that principally holds concentrated positions in large capitalization companies." (Pershing site)

The iShares Flexible Income & Opportunities ETF, which mainly invests in debt and income-producing securities, rose from 5% on 6/30/25 to 5.8% as of 9/30/25.

Saba Capital Income and Opportunities Fund, at 3.83% as of 6/30/25, was replaced in the Top 10 by Saba Capital Income and Opportunities Fund II, at 2.8%. Also gone from the top 10 were Nuveen Municipal Value Fund, Clough Global Opportunities Fund, and Calamos Long/Short Equity & Dynamic Income Trust.

New additions to the top 10 were Howard Hughes Holdings, Royce Value Trust, and Royce Micro Cap Trust, in a notable move toward smaller cap equities.

Earnings

RIV's fiscal year ends on 6/30. NII rose by ~3% in fiscal year ended 6/30/25. Net realized gains swung from a $6M loss to a $24.94M gain, while unrealized gains declined from a $38.28M gain to a $1.82M loss. NII, realized gains, and long-term gains covered 95% of common distributions in fiscal year ended 6/30/25, a marked improvement over the period ended 6/30/24, when coverage was nil, after accounting for preferred distributions.

Taxes

Management estimated that 45% of 2024 distributions were non-qualified, and 49% were non-dividend distributions, i.e., a return of capital. ROC offers a tax deferral, but it reduces your tax basis, which will impact your tax liability if you sell your shares.

Performance

As of 12/31/25, RIV had outperformed the Morningstar US CEF category over the past year. It lagged in price over the past three-, five- and 10-year periods but outperformed the category on an NAV basis:

RIV outperformed the category on an NAV basis in 2025, rising 18.18%, and also did so in 2023 and 2021. It also held up better in 2022 on both a price and NAV basis.

Risks

Interest rate risk: As seen above, rising interest rates pose a risk to RIV due to its exposure to BDCs - it fell 11% in price and NAV in 2022. Its fixed income exposure also has risks associated with rising rates.

SPAC risk: SPACs pose significant risks for investors, in that investors don't know which companies these entities may acquire. There can be a rapid timeline for mergers, which may lead to bad deals. In addition, there's less regulatory control over SPACs. Overall, SPACs have had a mixed record.

Valuations

Buying CEFs at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy due to mean reversion. CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day. As of the 1/21/26 close, RIV was trading at a 5.58% discount to its daily NAV/share of $12.55,which was a much deeper discount than its five-year average 1.94% discount. It was a similar discount to its three-year average of 5.53%, and a bit deeper than its one-year average of 4.26%:

Parting Thoughts

While RIV can offer you diversification via its holdings in many disparate assets, we advise waiting for a deeper discount to NAV. Its 52-week discount to NAV ranged as deep as 9.92%.

All tables are furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: Xiaomi Suffers EV/Smartphone Headwinds - Oversold Indicators Imply Trading Floor
data.description:
I previously covered Xiaomi Corporation (XIACY) (XIACF) in October 2025, discussing its robust growth prospects from the diversified ecosystem across the smart devices/EVs and the upcoming EV profitability.

Combined with the attractive valuations despite the triple-digit rally in 2024, the growing backlog/expanding production capacity, and the healthy balance sheet, I had initiated a Buy rating upon a moderate retracement then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the XIACY stock here, thanks to the improved margin of safety from the recent meltdown and the notably cheaper valuations compared to historical trends and its automaker/smartphone peers.

This is aided by their ability to weather the potential EV/smartphone downturn in 2026, attributed to their growing Free Cash Flow generations and the richer balance sheet, despite the risks from the lowered domestic EV tax exemption/trade-in subsidies in 2026, the intensifying domestic/international EV competition, and the impact of the memory chip supply crunch on its smartphone prospects.

XIACY Faces Near-Term EV Headwinds

For now, XIACY has unfortunately breached my prior Buy Zone at the intermediate support levels of $32s to retest the 2025 trading floor of $22s by the time of writing, with a similar pessimism also observed in its Chinese automaker peers in varying degrees.

The headwinds are partly attributed to:

  • the transition from full purchase tax exemption in 2025 to a 50% exemption from January 01, 2026 onwards - with "NEV buyers will pay 5% purchase tax starting this year,"
  • the further reduction to 25% exemption from January 01, 2027 onwards - 10% purchase tax, and
  • the transition from fixed-amount subsidies in 2025 to percentage-based subsidies relative to the vehicle price in 2026,

with it expected to trigger "a short-term sales surge as buyers rush to beat the deadline, followed by a potential slowdown in early 2026 once the reduced benefit takes effect."

The increase in China's EV retail sales has been observed at 1.337M units (+1.2% MoM/+2.6% YoY) in December 2025 along with 12.81M units in 2025 (+17.6% YoY), albeit with things notably cooling for the first three weeks of 2026 at -52% MoM/-16% YoY.

The same has been observed in XIACY's guidance, at which there may be a certain impact on its gross margins in FY2026, with interested investors well advised to monitor their future execution:

And regarding the vacancy tax, regarding next year's ASP and our gross margin, there will be certain impact. However, our current gross margin is at a healthy level. So we hope that while we can maintain or guarantee our delivery, we can still maintain a healthy gross margin.

(Xiaomi - FQ3'25 earnings call)

My caution is attributed to XIACY competitors' intensified discounting strategies, with it remaining to be seen if the former may join the potential price war if demand remains lackluster:

As deliveries failed to live up to expectations, all major players would have to slash prices of their vehicles to reduce their inventories at the beginning of 2026... Competition is getting fiercer this year because of weaker demand for new cars.

(South China Morning Post)

With XIACY set to report their FQ4'25 earnings call sometime in March 2026, I urge readers to closely monitor their upcoming performance and the management's forward commentary indeed.

This is because it is uncertain if the prior FQ3'25 Smart EV/AI segment profitability at gross margins of ~25.5% (+8.4 points YoY) and operating margins at approximately ~2.4% (+19.9 points YoY) may be replicated from FY2026 onwards.

This is especially since many of XIACY's competitors have highlighted their international EV growth ambitions to mitigate the maturing domestic market, with the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers expecting "vehicle exports are likely to rise 4.3% after a stronger-than-expected 21.1% growth in 2025."

Given XIACY's somewhat late global EV entrance from 2027 onwards and the potential domestic inventory glut at a time of mixed demand environment, I can understand why the management has offered a relatively conservative FY2026 EV delivery guidance of 550K units (+37.5% YoY).

2026 Brings Forth Smartphone Headwinds

Otherwise, readers must note that XIACY's main monetization avenue remains in the Smartphone segment at 40.6% of its overall revenues in FQ3'25 (-10.7 points YoY), with the ongoing data center memory supply crunch expected to trigger a downstream headwind in the supply and prices of smartphone memory chips.

For reference, International Data Corporation [IDC] previously reported that "for a mid-range device, memory can represent 15-20% of the total bill of materials (BOM), while for a high-end flagship device, it is around 10-15%," with these presenting headwinds to many smartphone OEMs, XIACY included.

My caution is attributed to the fact that XIACY is the third largest smartphone player globally with a market share of 13% (-1 point YoY) and the fourth largest smartphone player in China with a market share of 15.7% in 2025 (+0.7 points YoY).

While XIACY has been embarking on an intensified premiumization strategy in China, one that has been rather successful given that "our premium smartphone units sold accounted for 24.1% of our total smartphone units sold in the Chinese Mainland" (+4.1 points YoY), the same has not occurred overseas, as observed in the "decreased ASP in the overseas markets."

For now, XIACY already expects to mitigate some "cost pressure through product mix upgrades, ASP increases" while "digesting" some costs internally, with it likely to trigger near-term gross margin headwinds and, potentially, demand headwinds:

Regarding smartphone retail price, if it increases, then our judgment is that there will be a downturn in the overall smartphone market. That's my overall judgment. But how much? Well, of course, I can't really quantify in detail now. Now for smartphones, it is still a retention market for the time being.

(Xiaomi - FQ3'25 earnings call)

As a result of the potential top/bottom-line deterioration from the intensifying domestic EV competition, the delayed overseas EV launch, and the elevated memory prices impacting its smartphone prospects, it is unsurprising that XIACY has already lost -43.7% of its value since the 52-week highs.

XIACY Remains A Great Buy Here

For now, XIACY has already lost much of its 2025 stock price gains, with the stock currently retesting the support levels of $22s by the time of writing after running away from the 50/100/200 day moving averages.

It goes without saying that XIACY has already entered oversold territories by the time of writing, as observed in the trading volumes retesting prior 2021 lows.

The same has been observed in XIACY's discounted FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.56x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 19.08x, compared to the 1Y mean of 2.17x/28.90x and the sector median of 3.58x/25.28x, respectively.

When comparing XIACY's FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.56x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 19.08x to its smartphone/automaker peers, including:

  • Apple (AAPL) at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 8.01x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 30.09x,
  • Samsung (SSNLF) at 2.32x/25.35x,
  • Tesla (TSLA) at 15.50x/275.08x,
  • Li Auto (LI) at 0.34x/56.03x,
  • BYD Company Ltd. (BYDDY)(BYDDF) at 0.91x/21.77x,
  • XPeng (XPEV) at 1.46x/NA, and
  • NIO Inc. (NIO) at 1.10x/NA, respectively,

It is apparent that XIACY remains attractively valued at current levels, after the steep meltdown from recent 52 week highs, especially aided by the well-diversified Human x Car x Home smart ecosystem and the profitable growth trends.

These reasons may also be why the management has announced an outsized share repurchase program worth approximately ~$321M in early January 2026, as they acknowledge the cheap stock prices and deliver a robust shareholder return.

As a result of the potential bottoming at the $22s arising from the oversold technical indicators, the cheap valuations, and their ability to weather the near-term EV/smartphone headwinds thanks to the growing Free Cash Flow generation of 20.52B RMB on a YTD basis (+143.7% YoY) and the rich balance sheet with an estimated net cash position of 63.17B RMB (+22.3% YoY), I am reiterating my Buy rating for the XIACY stock here.

Much of the pessimism may already be baked in here.
